symptoms: Severe blistering, brown necrotic blisters; leaves fully necrose, curl, distort, and drop; stem distortion, bending, and dieback; affects >30% of shoots, leading to major defoliation and bush weakening; can cause 30-50% crop loss in epidemics. Widespread shoot dieback; secondary infections increase; common in high-altitude tea gardens (>1000m).
treatments: Urgent: Multiple systemic fungicides like tridemorph (0.05%) or contaf (0.1%) alternated with triazoles (e.g., hexaconazole) to avoid resistance; apply every 5-7 days for 2-3 weeks. Cultural: Heavy pruning of infected shoots (remove 15-20 cm below symptoms); regulate nitrogen fertilizers to avoid succulent growth. Consult experts for resistance checks; use bioagents consortia (Bacillus + Trichoderma) for long-term management.
llm_response:
Hello there, neighbor! As a tea disease assistant from Hatton, I'm here to help you understand Blister Blight. This disease is quite common in our high-altitude tea gardens.

**Symptoms:**

• Severe blistering on the leaves and stems
• Brown, necrotic blisters that can kill the plant tissue
• Leaves will curl, distort, and drop off
• Stem distortion and dieback
• Widespread shoot dieback
• Secondary infections increase

**Treatments:**

• Urgent: Use systemic fungicides like tridemorph or contaf every 5-7 days for 2-3 weeks to avoid resistance.
• Heavy pruning of infected shoots (remove 15-20 cm below symptoms)
• Regulate nitrogen fertilizers to prevent succulent growth
• Consult experts for resistance checks and use bioagents consortia for long-term management

**Practical tips:**

* Keep a close eye on your tea gardens, especially during peak season.
* Use a combination of fungicides and cultural practices for effective management.
* Share knowledge with fellow farmers in Hatton to build a stronger tea community!

Remember to always wear protective gear when handling fungicides. Wash your hands thoroughly after use. Stay safe!
